# Homecare Industry Snapshot — TheDealSheet

**Author:** TheDealSheet Editorial
**Source:** [thedealsheet.co](https://thedealsheet.co/industries/homecare)
**Saved:** 2026-04-20

## Why I Saved This

Homecare was previously rejected as a G&B niche. Captured 2026-04-20 to cross-reference TDS's PE-POV data against the rejection rationale — confirms whether the pass still holds or whether a specific sub-segment (private-pay / HNW concierge) could fit the buy-box. Feeds the TDS PE-POV cross-reference memo at [[traces/2026-04-20-tds-pe-pov-xref]].

## Content

### Market Size & Growth
- U.S. market: $155.9B–$162.35B (2024–2025 range; IBISWorld vs. Grand View)
- CAGR 2020–2025: 3.7% (IBISWorld)
- CAGR 2025–2033 forecast: 6.5%–10% (Grand View 10% / Fortune Business 7.4%)
- Demographic driver: 17.5% of U.S. population 65+ (~60M) → 22% by 2034

### Segment Mix
| Segment | Revenue Share | Gross Margin | Valuation Multiple |
|---|---|---|---|
| Skilled Nursing / Medical Home Health | 55% | 15–25% | 3x–6x EBITDA (strategic 9x–12x+) |
| Non-Medical Personal Care | 35% | 20–30% | 2.86x SDE avg / 5x–8x EBITDA at scale |
| Hospice & Palliative Care | 10% | 25–35% | 10x–15x EBITDA (highest margins) |

### Margins (G&B buy-box: $2–10M revenue)
| Metric | Industry | Top Quartile |
|---|---|---|
| Gross margin | 30–40% | 40–50% |
| EBITDA margin | 10–15% | 18–25% |
| Labor % revenue | 60–70% | 60–70% (optimized) |

Service-level pricing: Personal Care $25–$35/hr, Skilled Nursing $95–$165/visit, PT/OT $125–$200/visit, Hospice $175–$225/day.

### Valuation Multiples
| Revenue Band | Multiple | Metric |
|---|---|---|
| $500K–$1.5M | 1.96x–2.5x | SDE |
| $1.5M–$3M | 2.5x–3.0x | SDE |
| $3M–$5M | 2.8x–3.5x | SDE |
| $5M–$10M | 3.0x–4.5x SDE / 5x–8x EBITDA | Bridge |
| $10M+ / $5M+ EBITDA | 7x–15x | EBITDA |

Premium drivers: 30%+ private-pay/MA mix, <40% turnover, EHR + remote monitoring, diversified referral (5+ channels), multi-service continuum, geographic density, clean audit history, 75%+ renewal rate.

### Recurring Revenue & Retention
- Client retention: 70–80% industry / 85–90% top quartile
- Avg client tenure: 9–18 months / 24+ months top quartile
- Preferred renewal threshold: >75%
- Frequency models: daily/weekly (personal care) vs. episodic 60-day (Medicare)

### Growth Drivers
Aging-in-place (9 in 10 seniors prefer home), chronic disease (~50% of patients with 1+ condition), Medicare Advantage expansion with supplemental in-home benefits, Medicaid HCBS waiver funding shift from nursing homes, value-based care reducing medical costs 19–30%, telemonitoring adoption (68% of Medicare agencies).

### PE Activity
M&A pace: 2022 ~100 → 2023 ~100 → 2024 138 (+32% YoY) → 2025 H1 105 (on pace for +25% YoY). PE accounts for 50–60% of add-on acquisitions. Hospice record: 16 deals Q4 2025.

Named platforms:
- Altocare (Waud Capital Partners) — Senior Helpers 2024 + MedTec Healthcare merger April 2025
- Addus HomeCare (NASDAQ: ADUS) — $350M Gentiva personal care, $21.2M Helping Hands, $7.4M Del Cielo (2024–2025)
- Optum / UnitedHealth — $3.3B Amedisys acquisition Q3 2025 (divested 150+ locations post-DOJ review)
- Choice Health at Home (PE-backed) — trio of acquisitions 2025–2026
- Enhabit (Kinderhook Industries, $1.1B, Feb 2026) — 250 home health + 115 hospice locations
- Help At Home (PE-backed) — 3+ acquisitions 2023 (PA, GA, OH, IN)
- Elder Care Homecare (Rallyday Partners) — Northeast platform Q3 2025
- Havencrest Capital Management — 6–7 Texas bolt-ons 2024–2025

Strategic buyers also active: BrightSpring, Aveanna, Pennant, UnitedHealth.

### Operational Challenges
- Caregiver turnover: 79% annually (BLS 2024)
- Open positions: 765,800/yr unfilled; 9.7M total openings projected 2024–2034
- Wage pressure: median $34K–$35K HHA, $36–$40K CNA, $50–$55K LPN, $75–$85K RN
- Wage growth: 18% YoY
- 59% of agencies report caregiver shortages
- Compliance cost: $8K–$20K/yr
- Training pipeline: 2,000+ hrs OJT + 144 hrs classroom

Top-quartile labor strategies: wage premiums ($40K+) reduce turnover 35%, structured 90-day onboarding reduces early attrition 20–25%, career pathways (40% of top agencies offer).

### 2025–2026 Regulatory Changes
- 2026 Home Health PPS Final Rule: 1.3% payment reduction (vs. proposed 6.4% cut)
- HHCAHPS Survey Revision Q2 2026
- OASIS-E1 Update Q2 2026 (removed 4 assessment items)
- CMS API Interoperability Q1 2026 (payer API for prior auth/claims)
- EVV Expansion Q2 2026 (real-time visit documentation mandates)
- Senior Care Regulation Tightening Q1 2026 (enhanced background screening, transparency, continuing ed)

### Competitive Landscape
Fragmented independents ($500K–$5M) losing share to consolidators. Multi-service continuum (home health + personal care + hospice) becoming standard.

Geographic competition tiers:
- High competition (saturated, high wages): Tampa, Austin, NYC, SF
- Medium (growth markets, PE active): Phoenix, Dallas, Atlanta, Charlotte, Raleigh, Las Vegas
- Low (emerging): Jacksonville, Nashville
- Avoid: NYC ($50K+ wages), SF ($60K+ wages), Detroit (declining), Chicago proper (regulated)

Scale advantages: back-office consolidation cuts overhead 5–10%; wage standardization cuts turnover 35%.

### HNW / Luxury / Concierge Segment
Minimal explicit coverage. Closest signals:
- Private-pay segment commands premium multiples (5x–8x EBITDA vs. 3x–5x Medicaid-only)
- Affluent senior out-of-pocket spending: $4K–$8K/month willingness
- Long-term care insurance payouts as private-pay driver
- No explicit "luxury," "concierge," or "HNW-focused" sub-segment quantified in TDS data

## Key Patterns

- Pattern 1: **EBITDA margin floor (10–15% industry, 18–25% top quartile) fails G&B's 25%+ services-primary thesis** at the industry-wide level. Only hospice (25–35% gross, commanding 10x–15x EBITDA) clears the bar — and hospice is a specialized regulated clinical segment, not a services operation a search-fund principal runs.
- Pattern 2: **Labor is the binding constraint.** 79% caregiver turnover, 765K unfilled openings annually, 18% YoY wage growth. This is the opposite operational profile of premium-pest (sticky technicians) or coffee-equipment-servicing (skilled low-turnover tradespeople). Home-care is a labor-scarcity business by structural design.
- Pattern 3: **Payer mix determines valuation.** Medicaid-only operators trade at 3x–5x EBITDA; 30%+ private-pay/MA mix earns 5x–8x. The lever Kay would need to pull is concentration on private-pay clientele — but the industry data shows this is a minority sub-segment without quantified size.
- Pattern 4: **PE is aggressive (50–60% of add-ons, +32% YoY deal growth in 2024).** Named platforms span Waud, Kinderhook, Optum, Havencrest, Rallyday — the aggregator space is crowded. A $2–10M G&B platform would enter as a target for exit to these consolidators, not as a differentiated acquirer.
- Pattern 5: **Regulatory surface area is massive.** CMS rate cuts (1.3% for 2026), OASIS updates, EVV mandates, HHCAHPS revision, state senior-care tightening. Compliance cost $8–$20K/yr sits inside already-thin margins. G&B's thesis favors regulated-but-stable moats (licensed pest / HVAC) — homecare is regulated-and-volatile.
- Pattern 6: **No named HNW/concierge sub-segment in the PE-POV dataset.** The "private-pay concierge home care for HNW seniors" thesis exists in trade press but is not a TDS-tracked category. Size, margin profile, and multiple benchmarks for that sub-niche are not visible here — would require separate investigation, and absence from PE-POV coverage suggests it's not yet a consolidation-stage vertical.

## How I Might Use This

- **Confirms the prior rejection at the industry level.** EBITDA floor, labor structure, and regulatory exposure all fail G&B's buy-box on the aggregate market.
- **Flags one narrow revisit path:** private-pay concierge home care for HNW seniors (aging-in-place luxury). Unquantified in TDS — would need a separate one-pager round with primary research (HCAOA data, private-pay agency interviews) if Kay wants to pressure-test the sub-niche.
- **Not a DealsX next-wave candidate** (unlike commercial cleaning). Margin profile, labor crisis, and crowded PE aggregator field argue against reopening at any future trigger date.
- **Competitive exclusion list** for any future healthcare-adjacent niche: the 8 named PE platforms above are the aggregators any platform would compete against or exit to.
